Transcrição Completa: How To Craft The Perfect Show Rate Email Sequence - 6 Emails per day
[00:00]
All right, let me catch my breath real quick. Geez. Um, okay. So, to be clear, uh, this week, look, I know a lot of you guys requested tracking. However, I got to ask my clients permission to be able to share all our revenue numbers and, you know, all the customer data because we use a combination of CRM and in addition to that, we use Hyros.
[00:24]
So, Hyros will obviously show their revenue per channel and the client themselves. And then in addition to that, the CRM will show all the customer data. So, I'm most likely going to have to film that video on its own, like edit it to blur out different customer information and all that so I can actually share it with you guys transparently because at the end of the day, that's what it's going to take to likely get that video to you guys.
[00:50]
Um, to be clear, one request that we did have from Andrew this week was, "What do you actually cover inside of these six emails per day that go out when you are just absolutely hammering people who book calls in between the time they book and in between time that they show up?"
[01:09]
Um, to be clear, because Andrew was the one that requested it, we'll use Andrew's business as an example. Uh, Andrew, do you want to unmute yourself real quick and walk people through what the offer is that we're going to be doing this example for?
[01:22] ANDREW:
Yeah. Yeah. So, I sell or I teach people how to start and scale sober living homes in the sober living portfolio. So, we'll help people come in in six months. They'll start three, four, five sober living homes. They literally 10x their cash flow if they're already a real estate investor.
[01:42]
They'll go from 300-400 a month to 3,000-4,000. What we typically see with those is like 7 or 8,000 a month. So, it's a pretty solid offer. 8,800 is all it is. They'll make that back within their first 90 days operating. And I have three closers. And so we're looking at we have zero follow-up sequence right now and a very terrible pre-call sequence. So thank you for this.
[02:10]
Man, I could sit here and just fire off a bunch of stuff in addition to that too for what I was just talking about for how much more probable it is to make 400 after you do this, but I'll let that rest.
[02:23]
To be clear, everybody on this call, listen to what Andrew just said, and I want you in the chat right now to type what questions immediately come up about it. So, let's use the example you saw an ad. Andrew just used that specific hook where he explains what a sober living home is briefly.
[02:43]
He said something about you have to potentially be a real estate investor. I'm not sure what he said about that. He said something about this could 10x cash flow. Like, I assume you guys are going to have questions about what the he talks about with that. He said it was 8,800 bucks.
[03:01]
So like there's probably questions about how much money it's probable to make and when. Put whatever questions immediately come up about his offer into the chat right now. Take 30 seconds. Once again in the chat put whatever questions immediately come up about Andrew's offer that he just articulated.
[03:22]
He called it sober living homes. Now, Andrew, while they're typing their questions in, why don't you go ahead and tell us on average how long it takes for somebody to book a call and then actually have the call?
[03:35] ANDREW:
They uh you said, "How long to book a call?" So, if I go book a call right now, how long did it take for me to actually talk to you or talk to your salesperson?
[03:44]
We were booked out eight or nine days. People couldn't book for a while. They were writing comments on YouTube saying, "Your calendar's broken. I can't book," reaching out to me on DMs trying to find me.
[03:57]
With three closers and yourself taking calls, what will the timeline realistically be now?
[04:02] ANDREW:
So now it should be within 72 hours they can have that call.
[04:06]
Got it. Okay. Great. So let's look at a few of these questions and see if they'd be applicable for what we are trying to do to just reiterate. Okay. What we are trying to accomplish is somebody who books a call before they show up to that call. We usually have about 2 to three days between the time they book and the time they show up.
[04:29] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Gap in Sales Process"
NÚMEROS: 2-3 days between booking and call
VISUAL: Representação visual da linha do tempo entre agendamento e realização da call, destacando o período de 72 horas como janela crítica de engajamento
[04:32]
Now, a lot of businesses, they're terrible with how they think this works. They generally think that when somebody books a call, they're sold on showing up. It's not true. When somebody books a call, they're essentially interested enough in the moment to further explore what you're ideally going to sell them on a call.
[04:53]
So the point of the sales call is obviously to become sold. So if the point of the sales call is to become sold, but yet regularly businesses and salespeople constantly complain about, oh, people show up to my calls and they're not ready to be sold yet. Where's the gap there, right?
[05:13]
Where's the gap that needs filled? It's pretty simple. It's obviously the pre-call sequence. If closers want people to show up ready to actually buy, we have to take the lead and sell them before the call actually happens.
[05:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Pre-Call Sequence Purpose"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Diagrama mostrando a necessidade de "vender antes da call", com setas indicando o fluxo de informação através de emails e conteúdo
[05:30]
We have to answer all their questions. We have to go through all the framing with them and we just have to go through pretty much like a Q&A sequence with these people and we have to anticipate and guess and use the feedback from our salespeople to be able to leverage that into an email sequence and into a content sequence as well that we can use to hammer people.
[05:53]
To make sure that our cashiers aka our salespeople can actually fulfill their dream of having a lead show up and just close and give them a credit card. Okay, to be clear, let's go through a few of these questions that we see here and let's see if they could work as emails.
[06:11]
Okay, so Anthony says, "How much extra management is required compared to traditional real estate?" So this question is rather expansive in terms of how it can be answered. How he interpreted what you said, Andrew, is well, is this a real estate investment?
[06:28]
Like are you comparing it to section 8? Are you comparing it to commercial? Are you comparing it to residential? Are you comparing it to rentals? Are you comparing it to apartments? Are you comparing it to investing? And the reason that thought was prompted was because you mentioned something about being a real estate investor.
[06:46]
You see, so to be clear as an entire email, go ahead and just answer it real quick for us. Do you have to be a real estate investor? Is this a real estate investment? Is it not a real estate investment? What would you say to that?
[06:59] ANDREW:
We are actively operating real estate. So, it is a real estate investment, but you can do rental arbitrage as well. So, you can buy or you can lease, but we do control the real estate and manage the business.
[07:13]

[11:37]
Absolutely, they need to know the price of the offer. And they need to know how you accept payment. Some people out there only accept wires. Some people accept credit cards. Some people can set you up with financing if you need to and stretch it out over like five years in monthly payments if you wanted to do it that way.
[11:55]
Okay? So, they need to know the price of the offer, how they can buy it. They need to know how much money they're coming out of pocket when it's an investment related opportunity.

NÚMEROS: Não especificado

[12:07]

[12:21]
Like, well, what's creative financing? How does that work? Why would somebody want to do that in comparison to getting a cash offer or a loan? Like, what's their incentive to do that?

NÚMEROS: 6 emails per day, 2 questions covered so far

[12:48]
Do we want to do the stupid "Hey, you have a call coming up on Wednesday at 3 p.m." Is that what is that what we're talking about doing? No, we're talking about doing actually valuable Q&A based questions that people want to know that they would talk about on a sales call that they get to know before the sales call.
[13:08]
That way, your salespeople can be their dream a cashier and just take somebody's credit card by the time they show up. Does that make sense?
[13:17]
In addition to that, you keep hearing me say this content stuff. So, it's not just emails, okay? It's six emails a day. Absolutely. Okay. But in addition to that, we want to make sure that we're making content on all this.

Emails (6x/dia)
Instagram Reels
Facebook cross-posts
"Hammer Them Strategy" Setas conectando todos os canais em abordagem integrada
[13:33]
So like Andrew, to be clear, all these email ideas are also short form pieces of content that you are going to post on Instagram reels that you cross-post to Facebook that you are going to use the hammer them strategy for that I just posted into the group chat the other day. Does that make sense?
[13:49] ANDREW:
Yeah.
[13:50]
Okay. That way we get in front of them on all angles. Now sometimes, just take a moment, people say, "Oh, six emails too much." Go ahead everybody, just real quick. If you were considering investing into a sober living home, wouldn't you want all this information before a call so you know if you're wasting your time or not?
[14:10]
Like, wouldn't you actually, once again, if you're genuinely interested in doing this, wouldn't you want to know these things? It's like I use the example when I talk about the six emails a day of this company that claimed they could get rid of my income tax in the United States by creating an entity in Dubai.
[14:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Dubai Tax Strategy Case Study"
NÚMEROS: 0% income tax, 0 emails sent
VISUAL: Exemplo pessoal ilustrando falha de follow-up: empresa prometendo eliminação de impostos através de entidade em Dubai, mas sem sequência de emails educacionais
[14:32]
Bundling all my money through that Dubai entity and therefore paying 0% income tax. They provided me zero emails in between the time I saw that claim and the time of the call. Therefore, I did not show up.
[14:47]
If that company would have sent me 12 emails a day about other people who have done it, how it technically works, how the IRS doesn't me, what my probability of audit would be if I did something like that, how my business entity in the United States is now handled, and how I report on it.
[15:10]
Like, can you also think of infinite questions that you'd love to have answered if you were actually considered solving that? And it's like, dude, that problem is a expensive problem that I desperately want to fix. I hate taxes and I pay so much in taxes.
[15:26]
Okay, my point being, dude, that'd be I would have I would have consumed at a minimum six emails a day from those guys if they would have hit me with six. I would have loved to see like 20 pieces of content a day from those people about people who have done it, all the questions that I have and learning about the process in real time. Do you get what I'm saying?
[15:49] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Ideal Information Consumption"
NÚMEROS: 6-12 emails per day desired, 20+ pieces of content
VISUAL: Representação do apetite de informação de um prospect verdadeiramente interessado, mostrando disposição para consumir alto volume de conteúdo educacional
[15:51]
Okay, let's continue. Chris asked price, how long for results, case studies. Okay, so to be clear, he wants to know the price of it. He also wants to know how long it takes, which I also had as a question like how long does it take to actually make money with it. Those are the expectations related questions.
[16:13] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Four Email Categories Framework"
NÚMEROS: 4 categorias principais
VISUAL: Framework organizacional em quatro quadrantes:
Objections (Objeções)
General Questions (Perguntas Gerais)
Questions from Answers (Perguntas derivadas)
Expectations (Expectativas)
[16:15]
You see, so it's like categorically we have a few things that we can consider when we're crafting our six emails a day and our content for the hammer them strategy. We have the objections. We have the general questions. We have the questions that come from those questions getting answered. And we have expectations.
[16:33]
Okay, those are our four categories. Objections, the general questions, the questions that come from those questions being answered, and our expectations. Okay, go ahead and just real quick look in the Zoom chat here, read the questions and tell me they all don't fit into those four categories.
[16:52]
Okay? Or like I talked about when somebody had a question and you answered it, what immediately happened after? Hey, does anybody else have questions now that you answered that? And then you got new questions that weren't in the chat yet. See how that works?
[17:07]
Now, how would this go if these questions weren't answered once again and somebody just showed up to a sales call? Well, all the questions would have to get answered on the sales call then. It's that simple, right? So, instead, we do all that pre-call.
[17:21]
Okay. Now, in an ideal world, would every single person consume all of these questions before the call? Yeah. Will they? Probably not. However, about 50% will.
[17:33] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Email Open Rate Statistics"
NÚMEROS: 50% open rate típica, 50% dos leads bem preparados
VISUAL: Gráfico de pizza mostrando divisão 50/50 entre leads que consomem todo conteúdo pré-call versus aqueles que não consomem, com destaque para taxa de abertura consistente
[17:35]
I regularly see 50% open rates on every single email in my six email a day sequences. Regular. It's about half your leads that come specifically from paid ads in this case or wherever they're coming from will show up much much better framed in comparison to the other half that just won't have consumed all of these. Does that make sense?
[17:58] ANDREW:
Yeah.
[17:59]

[18:18]
Of course not. Um, to be clear, that's why we do the emails. And Michael already knows this. I mean, he runs VSSL traffic as well. You you you any one of you right now refute me if you disagree.
[18:34]
When have you ever seen a VSSL that has 100% play rate and 100% retention rate of that video? Anybody. And please for the love of God if you have it share it with all of us. Right? It's just not how it works.
[18:49] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "VSSL Analytics - New Client Example"
NÚMEROS:
22% play rate
255 total views
204 started
51 at 8 minutes
Drop-off at 29 minutes
VISUAL: Gráfico de retenção Wistia mostrando curva descendente dramática, ilustrando queda típica de visualização em VSSLs, com destaque para pontos críticos de abandono
[18:51]
So, look at this as an example. This is a new client. Jeremy just referred me. This client, they have a 22% play rate, which means that out of every person that hits the page, two people are pressing play. Eight are not.
[19:07]
Okay, this is the retention out of 255 total views. Look at the quantity of people that start it. Okay, 204. Look at the quantity of people that are still there 8 minutes in. 51.
[19:22]
After you get to about it seems like 29 minutes in, there's a good majority that stick around from there, right? You see that how that retention line doesn't drop that much. But even then, I mean, you're still getting people that drop off. It's just not as bad.
[19:37] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Ideal vs Reality - VSSL Retention"
NÚMEROS: 29 minutos = ponto de estabilização
VISUAL: Comparação entre curva ideal (linha plana após 29 min) versus realidade (continua caindo), demonstrando que mesmo em melhores cenários há drop-off contínuo
[19:39]
If the entire video looked like this from from essentially that 29 minute mark forward, that'd be ideal. That would for sure work. That'd make a lot more sense. But here's how they always look. I mean, this is typically how it always works.
[19:54]
So, does that mean that those people won't book calls? No, of course not. That's not how human beings work. That's not how human beings work. Human beings, what we do is we attempt to conserve our energy.
[20:07] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Human Energy Conservation Psychology"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Conceito evolutivo ilustrado: humanos conservam energia por instinto de sobrevivência, aplicado ao comportamento de consumo de conteúdo
[20:09]
It's an evolutionary trait, okay? Because we used to have to scavenge for food and didn't have infinite food that we could eat all the time. So, what we constantly try to do is just conserve our energy and we only try to use it when necessary.
[20:24]
To be clear, soon as somebody thinks they understand something, you think they're going to sit there and continue consuming it all? No. Soon as in this case, people are sold enough, they say, "Okay, yeah, I'm interested in talking to them." And they fill out the application, they book a call, and then they get on the phones with your team.
[20:43]
So that's how it actually works. So that's why we need to hammer people in between the time they book and in between the time they show up. Nobody ever sits there and watches 100% of your videos and then you know show everybody knows that who runs call funnels here.
[21:00]
You know 100%. If you get a lead like that, you feel blessed. You know what I mean?
[21:05] PARTICIPANT (ANTHONY):
Jeremy, do you mind if I add something really quickly?
[21:08]
Yeah, of course. Go ahead.
[21:10] ANTHONY:
Um, so sales manager here, one thing that you can do for context to improve the consumption of your videos before the call is have your sales reps actually call them.
[21:20] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Sales Rep Pre-Call Strategy"
NÚMEROS: Aumento na taxa de consumo de assets
VISUAL: Processo de call de confirmação: rep liga → constrói rapport → confirma recebimento de vídeo → obtém compromisso de consumo antes da call principal
[21:22]
So, we have sales reps call leads as soon as they're booked in and basically just like build a little bit of rapport, have a quick conversation with them, make sure they have the video, and basically get confirmation with them that they're going to consume the assets before the call.
[21:38]
Don't like you don't want to make it sound like homework. You want to make it sound like they're obviously going to get a ton of value and a ton of information from it, but it's going to help you guys maximize the call that you have scheduled tomorrow. So, that's something that we do. It works super well. Really increases the amount of people that actually show up to the call actually being informed and having gone through all the assets.
[22:04]
Yeah. And to be clear, same thing as you all know like what's the connection rate going to be of a closer actually establishing contact before the call? It's not going to be 100%. What's the quantity of people that they do connect with actually consuming all the information? Not going to be 100%.
[22:22] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Multi-Angle Attack Strategy"
NÚMEROS: <100% connection rate, <100% consumption rate
VISUAL: Diagrama de abordagem multi-canal mostrando:
Sales rep calls
Hammer them strategy
6 emails per day Todos trabalhando simultaneamente para maximizar engajamento
[22:24]
That's why we hit people from all angles. When you have sales people like what Anthony just articulated, that helps a fuckload. But to be clear, that's why we need to also do the hammer them strategy. That's also why we need to do six emails a day.
[22:38]
All right, cool. Well, just to be rather direct, I mean, that's really it. We could sit here and use another example if you guys want, but it's pretty simple. Okay, now to be fair in this case, just in terms of argumentative, well, how do you do this if you don't get a bunch of people like what Andrew just had that asked him a bunch of questions?
[22:59] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Question Generation Sources"
NÚMEROS: 4 fontes principais
VISUAL: Fluxograma de fontes para gerar perguntas:
Sales team feedback
Most common objections
Organic lead insights
Group brainstorming sessions
[23:01]
It's pretty simple. You talk to your salespeople and you say, "Hey guys, like what are the most common questions people have when they get on the phones with us? What are you guys most frustrated that you have to repeat to people again and again and again?"
[23:16]
Like what for the people who do show up sold? Like what do they mention that you know they kind of reference that makes them say like, "Yeah, I consumed like this video or that video and like it helped a lot before me showing up here today."
[23:31]
You know, for the organic leads, it's like, well, they typically consumed a lot of videos. If you get an organic lead, they're obviously very happy to talk to your organization as is.
[23:41]
Try to get your sales people to start asking some questions to those people and specifically ask like if you don't mind me asking like what video specifically stuck out to you the most that made this like a no-brainer to you, you know.
[23:56]
Um, and to be clear, remember what you want to do is just go through the sales team's feedback. Okay? And in addition to that, you can kind of anticipate some questions that people might have. Okay?
[24:08]
Lastly, you could do exactly what we did here today. You can get a group of people together and you can ask them, "Hey, so this is my offer. This is typically what people would see inside of an ad. What questions immediately come up?
[24:23] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Four Categories Framework (Repeated)"
NÚMEROS: 4 categorias, 72 horas = 18 emails
VISUAL: Framework reforçado com cálculo matemático:
Objections
Questions
Questions from Questions
Expectations = 6 emails/dia × 3 dias = 18 emails totais
[24:25]
And just remember the categories. Objections, questions, questions that come from those questions getting answered, and expectations. Okay?
[24:33]
When you generally just think with those four categories, it's also very easy to kind of prompt yourself in order to come up with the questions that need answered. If you have 72 hours, you need a total of 18 emails. It's not that hard.
[24:48]
And once again, every email becomes a piece of content as well for the hammer them strategy. So you do those two things succinct together in order to maximize show rate and in order to maximize close rate. That's the two statistics that this specifically impacts the most. Does that make sense to everybody?
[25:08]
Okay. Does anybody have questions or any general just like Anthony Gabe? Anybody have anything they want to contribute? Go ahead. What do you got?
[25:17] PARTICIPANT:
Question. So in 24 hours it is six emails, 48 is 12 and so on. Is there any way that we prioritize some category if we have just 24 hours for example compared to...
[25:33]
That's a very fair question you can treat it kind of like a sales process so typically what comes up first is the questions and then the questions from those questions and then the objections and then the expectations.
[25:47] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Email Prioritization Sequence"
NÚMEROS: Ordem 1-2-3-4
VISUAL: Fluxo sequencial de priorização espelhando processo de vendas: 1st → General Questions 2nd → Questions from Answers 3rd → Objections 4th → Expectations
[25:49]
Great question. Thank you for that. Sanjay you got a question? We do another one?
[25:54]
What what'd you say?
[25:56]
Can we do another example?
[25:58]
Yeah, sure. Uh, let me answer a few questions real quick and then we can do that. Uh, Sanjay, do you have a question?
[26:04] SANJAY:
Yeah. When it comes to like actually setting this up in terms of the workflow, are you doing it through the ESP or through Calendly?
[26:13]
No, not Calendly. Uh, Calendly will typically leave for the reminder based emails. And to be clear, just very direct, we will do the six emails in addition to the reminder emails that are sent by calendar in this case.
[26:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Email System Architecture"
NÚMEROS: 6 educational emails + Calendly reminders
VISUAL: Diagrama de sistemas separados:
Calendly → Reminder emails (tempo/logística)
CRM (Active Campaign/GoHighLevel) → 6 educational emails Trabalhando em paralelo, não substituindo um ao outro
[26:30]
So, I'm talking in addition just for everybody who's listening to this. Uh, but yeah, we'll do them through whatever the CRM is like active campaign or whatever is being used. Go high level.
[26:42] SANJAY:
So, it's able to like pull the date of the call and then let's say like their call is for Thursday. If they would book on Wednesday, it's going in relation to the time of the call, not when they enter the workflow. Because if you set up like a standard flow, it'd be like you get hit an hour after, two hours after, three hours after. It's working backwards from the time of the call, right?
[27:06]
Great question. So, it depends on what your CRM is going to enable you to do or not do. Some CRM unfortunately will not enable you to like import the actual time of the call and work marketing automation specific around that.
[27:21]
You just in that case would want to take the average of how long it takes for somebody to book a call and actually have the call. If that's 72 hours as an example, you'd have logically three days. You'd send six emails, six emails, and six emails.
[27:36] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "CRM Limitation Workaround"
NÚMEROS: 72 hours average = 3 days = 18 emails (6+6+6)
VISUAL: Solução simplificada mostrando:
Opção A: CRM avançado (trabalha backwards da call)
Opção B: CRM básico (usa média de 72h, envia 6/dia) Ambas funcionam efetivamente
[27:38]
So regardless of whether you can import the call time or not, or you want to make it that advanced or simplify it a bit, you'd be fine regardless just by doing six a day. If once again, you have a three-day average for somebody to actually get on the call.
[27:54] SANJAY:
And just like ballpark, I'm just curious, what kind of bumps in like show rate have you seen because of this? And where do you draw the line in terms of it like being spammy? Because I have a client who they do this, they just don't do this well. Like all the emails kind of suck, but they do hit them like four or five times a day and they got a lot of complaints about it being spammy. So like I guess both I guess that's two questions.
[28:18]
Well, first of all, think out loud about what you just said. If you send spammy emails, of course people are going to complain about it being spammy.
[28:27] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Spam vs Education Distinction"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Comparação lado a lado: ❌ SPAM: "Your call is tomorrow!" ✓ EDUCATION: "How Dubai entities eliminate US tax legally" Mostrando diferença entre lembretes vazios e conteúdo valioso
[28:29]
If I send education-based emails like I used in the Dubai tax example, I would not in any way, shape, or form be complaining. I literally want that amount of information from the business so once again I can ensure I'm maximizing my time with that business. Does that make sense?
[28:47] SANJAY:
Yeah. So it's just more... And are they like typically shorter? Like you're just answering the one question in the email as concisely as possible then just move it to the next email after that?
[28:59]
Fair question, but no, they are not always short. Typically they are very like thorough and well built out.
[29:07]

[29:09]

[29:17]

[29:45]

[30:39]

[30:45] PARTICIPANT:
Okay, cool. Thank you.
[30:47]
Just to be very transparent, I've not seen it below 45%. When this is done properly, specifically with the opening email, generally you'll get about a 45-50% open rate for every email that follows.

Minimum target: 40% open rate
Typical performance: 45-50% open rate

[31:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Testimonial Integration Strategy"
NÚMEROS: Andrew's case: $110K → $200K → $400K/month trajectory
VISUAL: Framework de integração de case studies:
NÃO: Email standalone de depoimento
SIM: Case study tecido dentro de resposta a pergunta específica Mostrando como usar prova social para responder objeções/expectativas
[31:32]
Okay, so let's use what Andrew said at the beginning of the call just to be clear for anybody watching this recording. You know, we don't generally record any of the weekly wins, but Andrew was talking about the fact he's going to make 110K this month.
[31:47]
He added three new closers, and I asked him, I was like, "Well, what's the income goal for next month now that you got three additional closers?" and he said some stupid silly low money like $200,000 a month.
[32:01]
I immediately started pressing him on making $300,000 a month next month. And I demonstrated to him that it was only two extra deals per closer a week, which is very realistic.
[32:13]
This is also filmed on September 21st. So he has nine full days, technically eight and a half, including today, that enable him to ramp up the closers between now and the end of the month.
[32:26]
So they have a heightened probability to get two deals a week going into October. There's a very high probability that Andrew will exceed that super weak $200,000 goal that he had for next month.
[32:38]
And even greater than that, we showed how easy it was mathematically for him to hit $400,000 next month. So, as an example, I could then inside of a like let's use the example like a client acquisition funnel for our agency, okay?
[32:54] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Case Study Email Example Structure"
NÚMEROS:
Starting point: $110K/month
Conservative goal: $200K/month
Pushed goal: $300K/month
Stretch goal: $400K/month
Timeline: September 21st, 8.5 days to optimize
VISUAL: Estrutura narrativa de email usando case study de Andrew:
Setup: Client hitting plateau
Challenge: Pushed beyond comfort zone
Math: Apenas 2 deals/closer/week = $400K
Lesson: Importância de pensar maior
CTA: Aplicar para programa
[32:56]
Where I'm demonstrating okay, hey, like you want to work with our business, like fill out this form or whatever, okay? And then people have the time between the call and when they book the call.
[33:07]
I could use that exact example. Let's use the example. We fast forward a month. Andrew exceptionally surpassed $200,000 a month. Obviously, he will. And to be clear, he gets to the point where he'll get like 300 or maybe even $400,000. Okay.
[33:23]
We then get like a testimonial from Andrew saying, "Yeah, man. I'm so glad I got Jeremy up my ass about making more money. It's so obvious how much more money I should be making." And I thought I could make $200,000. He pressed the out of me and showed me mathematically how easy it was to get to 400,000.
[33:41]
I mean, dude, it's no-brainer to work with Jeremy. I've been in the program for like two months and look how much my income already jumped.
[33:48]
So, it's like that on its own. Sure, I could put that into an email, but like now imagine once again if I'm demonstrating as like answering a question like, well, what does it take to actually increase the probability of hitting a million dollar month?
[34:04] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Expectation-Setting Email with Embedded Proof"
NÚMEROS: Target: $1M/month
VISUAL: Email structure layering:
Question: "What does it take to hit $1M/month?"
Answer: Mindset, hunger, aggression, thinking big
Proof: Andrew's story woven throughout
Contrast: Moving slow vs moving fast Demonstrando como responder expectativa + provar com case study simultaneamente
[34:06]
That's like the question of the email. And I start answering like the expectations around what type of like mindset like hunger and aggression and like thinking big and like he said some about moving slow. It's like no, we eradicate that when we're trying to hit million dollar months.
[34:22]
We are tweaking out trying to hit a million dollars a month, like moving fast. You know what I mean? So, it's like I could break down that expectation and work in that case study of Andrew at the same time as an example versus if I just did the case study on its own. It's not as effective in comparison to answering and working as an example. You see what I'm saying?
[34:44]
So, ideally, you find strategic ways to work them into the categories. Like, the objection ones are typically the easiest. Uh, oh yeah, we had such and such student that wanted to start a sober living home, but they didn't have the $8,800.
[35:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Objection Email with Case Study Template"
NÚMEROS: $8,800 program cost, financing over 5 years, 6 months to ROI
VISUAL: Template de email de objeção:
Objection: "Don't have $8,800"
Story: Student com mesma objeção
Solution: Financing partner, 5-year terms
Result: 6 months → múltiplas propriedades + lucro cobrindo dívida + juros Demonstrando superação de objeção com prova real
[35:02]
They seemed like it was a lot of money to them, but they understood the value of creative financing. So, therefore, they knew they needed to figure out a way to get the money. They ended up doing with a little bit of interest one of our financing partners and spreading out the monthly payments over five years.
[35:18]
And within the first six months of the person being in the program after investing into themselves with a financing opportunity even though they had a lot more interest on the 8800. So they were paying more for the program than somebody who could just pay cash.
[35:32]
They ended up in six months making this much money and having this many sober living homes comfortably affording the debt justifying all the interest on the financing option that they picked. See what I'm saying?
[35:44]
So, it's like work it in and answer things at the same time. That's typically a best practice. Okay, great. Um, to be clear, I don't have a substantial amount of time left. I have a lunch I've got to go to here in about 25 minutes, but Chris requested us to do another example and I'll happily do that for us here.

Community activity level?

In addition to that, just a general question. Um, go ahead and answer that one, Chris. If it is organic, it seems slow. How fast can you get an additional 50 leads?
[39:07] CHRIS:
That's because it is slow. Um, a lot of these people, especially in my corner on LinkedIn, have no clue and absolutely no desire to run ads. Um, and I think even by saying anything other than organic, a lot of them would run a mile.
[39:26]
Um, myself included, even up to a year ago, how fast can you get an additional 50 leads? Depends on the strength of your network, your daily activity, um, and also, you know, your experience so far, client results, that kind of thing. Um, fluffy answer. I know.
[39:46]

[40:09]

[40:22] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Vague Answers Generate More Questions"

[40:24]
Yeah. Okay.
[40:26]
Okay. I then asked I said uh who's it for? So just knowing like who your usual buyer is. Uh that can always help you know somebody understand whether it's technically for them or not. Also weed out the wrong people.
[40:41]
Uh make the people who it is for technically feel better that they're in the funnel and excited for the call. Um Darren then asked a pretty similar question essentially just with a more specific way of asking it. Is this for newbies?

[42:26] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Commitment & Time Investment Questions"
NÚMEROS: 6-month program duration

Daily time commitment
Weekly activity requirements
Course consumption timeline

[42:28]

[42:38]

[42:53]
Um, people always ask well, like, "Is Jeremy available in the chat?" like, you know, when they buy one of our other courses and we show them like people tagging me, asking questions, like me going in and answering and stuff just as another example of how you can leverage that inside of emails.
[43:09]
Uh, okay. And then the final question was, I still don't understand why anyone would do this. Probably a who question. You see, so look at it like that.
[43:20] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Fundamental Clarity Gap"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Problema identificado: "I still don't understand why anyone would do this" ↓ Indica falta de clareza sobre:
Value proposition
Target market
Problem being solved
Unique mechanism
[43:22]
Like that's essentially the amount of time that you articulated yourself is actually longer than what a usual person would sit there and listen inside of an ad. But to be fair, like people make really fast judgments.
[43:35]
That's why the drop off rate in VSSLs is generally pretty steep because what they try to do is they try to figure out rapidly like, is this for me? Is this not for me? Um, is this something that can actually help me? Am I actually interested in doing this? Is the result appealing enough?
[43:52]
And like I said, they're going to conclude fast. They're never going to consume all of it. And that's where a lot of these emails can come in. Think about it like this. Somebody like Brandon might actually come through your funnel and book a call just to get on a call and then see if it's actually for him or not.
[44:09]
You know, because he might be interested enough like maybe Brandon knows somebody who's killing it on LinkedIn. So, he's like, "Well, I mean, this could work, but like I don't know if this guy's necessarily doing what that other guy's doing, you know, and he's going to show up and be like, "Are you doing what this other guy's doing? Like, are you actually teaching me that?" Like, you know, if so, I'm interested. If not, I don't know if it's for me. You know what I mean, right?
[44:36] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Unqualified Lead Behavior Pattern"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Jornada de lead não-qualificado:
Vê resultado de alguém no LinkedIn
Fica curioso se programa ensina aquilo
Agenda call para descobrir
Chega sem contexto
Faz perguntas básicas
Desperdiça tempo do closer
[44:38]
Okay, cool.
[44:40] CHRIS:
Yeah. Really helpful. Got like I don't know 40-50 ideas down.
[44:45]
Okay, cool. All right, sweet. Well, hope this helped. Excited to see everybody absolutely hammer the out of leads that come through their call funnels and uh make a lot more money as a result. Okay, talk soon.
[44:58] PARTICIPANTS:
Cheers, boys.
📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO
NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:
6 emails por dia durante período pré-call
72 horas = tempo médio entre booking e call = 18 emails totais
50% open rate consistente em todas as sequências
20-40% aumento em show rate (exemplo: 50% → 80%)
45-50% open rate mínimo quando executado corretamente
22% play rate típico em VSSLs
204 → 51 viewers aos 8 minutos (drop-off típico)
Andrew's case: $110K → $200K → $400K/mês trajetória
Programa exemplo: $8,800 (sober living homes)
ROI esperado: 90 dias para recuperar investimento
3 closers adicionados ao time de Andrew
ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:
Sequência de 6 Emails/Dia:
Emails educacionais (não lembretes)
Baseados em Q&A antecipado
Conteúdo robusto e detalhado
Enviados via CRM (Active Campaign/GoHighLevel)
Paralelo aos emails de lembrete do Calendly
Hammer Them Strategy:
Cross-posting em múltiplas plataformas
Instagram Reels
Facebook
Cada email = 1 peça de conteúdo short-form
Multi-Angle Attack:
6 emails/dia
Hammer them strategy (social content)
Sales rep pre-call outreach
VSSL/long-form content
Sales Rep Pre-Call Process:
Ligar assim que lead agenda
Build rapport rápido
Confirmar recebimento de assets
Obter compromisso de consumo
Frame como valor (não homework)
ESTRUTURA - 4 CATEGORIAS DE EMAIL:
Questions (Perguntas Gerais)
Primeiro a ser abordado
Perguntas óbvias sobre a oferta
Clarificações básicas
Questions from Questions (Perguntas Derivadas)
Segundo a ser abordado
Perguntas que surgem das respostas
Aprofundamento necessário
Objections (Objeções)
Terceiro a ser abordado
Preço, tempo, garantias
Riscos percebidos
Expectations (Expectativas)
Último a ser abordado
Timeline de resultados
Compromisso necessário
Probabilidade de sucesso
FONTES PARA GERAR PERGUNTAS:
Feedback do sales team
Objeções mais comuns
Insights de leads orgânicos
Sessões de brainstorming em grupo
Análise de drop-off em VSSLs
INTEGRAÇÃO DE CASE STUDIES:
❌ NÃO: Email standalone de depoimento
✅ SIM: Tecido dentro de resposta a pergunta/objeção
Usar para responder expectativas
Demonstrar superação de objeções
Validar processo/timeline
DADOS FINANCEIROS:
Show rate improvement: 30 percentage points (50% → 80%)
Email open rate: 45-50% consistente
VSSL play rate: 22% típico
VSSL retention: Steep drop (204 → 51 aos 8min)
Connection rate de sales reps: <100%
Consumption rate após conexão: <100%
ROI típico: Recuperação em 90 dias (caso Andrew)
PRINCÍPIOS-CHAVE:
Leads não estão "vendidos" ao agendar - apenas interessados
Gap crítico: entre booking e call
Closers querem leads prontos para comprar
Pre-call sequence = responsabilidade de vender antes da call
Spam vs Educação: Conteúdo valioso nunca é spam
Conservação de energia: Humanos param de consumir quando "entendem o suficiente"
100% consumption/retention: Impossível e não necessário
50% de engajamento = resultado excelente
ERROS COMUNS IDENTIFICADOS:
Enviar apenas lembretes de horário
Assumir que booking = comprometimento
Respostas vagas que geram mais incerteza
Não definir avatar claramente
Case studies isolados (não integrados)
Depender 100% de VSSL para educação
Esperar 100% de consumption rate
DURAÇÃO: 44:58 FORMATO: Training call / Workshop ao vivo com Q&A DATA: 21 de Setembro, 2024 APRESENTADOR: Jeremy (Agency owner) PARTICIPANTES: Andrew (Sober Living), Chris (LinkedIn Organic), Anthony (Sales Manager), Sanjay, Drew, Darren, Bohan, Brandon, outros membros
